text: The surgeon performs splenic flexure mobilization using a medial to lateral dissection technique, carefully separating the tissue planes to expose the splenic flexure.
The surgeon gains access below the inferior mesenteric vein, carefully preserving retroperitoneal structures, and proceeds with medial to lateral dissection during the splenic flexure mobilization.
After completing the dissection, the surgeon places a swab to protect the retroperitoneal structures. The inferior mesenteric vein is then ligated and divided.
The surgeon makes an entry into the lesser sac from above the pancreas, revealing the anatomical space and surrounding structures.
The surgeon confirms entry into the lesser sac by visualizing the posterior wall of the stomach, which is clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon attempts to gain entry into the lesser sac from above the transverse colon, focusing on the anatomical positioning relative to the colon.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers to avoid disrupting the transverse mesocolon during the procedure.
The surgeon completes the dissection up to the spleen, following the entry into the lesser sac from above the pancreas.

The surgeon observes patchy ischemia in the transverse colon, which is being retracted cranially. This maneuver reveals a significant defect in the transverse mesocolon, indicating disruption of the blood supply to the transverse colon.
The surgeon mobilizes the transverse colon up to the hepatic flexure, carefully manipulating the colon to separate it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon assesses the transverse colon, noting that the specimen will need to be resected proximally due to the observed ischemia and mesocolon defect.
The surgeon focuses on the area proximal to the ischemic region of the transverse colon, assessing the extent of tissue affected by disrupted blood supply.
The surgeon confirms that the transverse colon, affected by patchy ischemia and a defect in the transverse mesocolon, can no longer reach the pelvis.
The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection to take the inferior mesenteric artery, completing the dissection to mobilize the entire left colon.
A sizer is inserted into the rectum to ensure that a surgical stapling device can be positioned high within the rectum.
The surgeon prepares the rectum for transection, indicating the next step in the surgical procedure.
The surgeon extracts the specimen following the completion of right colon mobilization, indicating the removal of the mobilized section of the colon.
The surgeon extracts the specimen and performs excision. The colonic segment is then passed back into the abdomen through a glove port.
The surgeon places an anvil into the colonic end, preparing for the next step in the anastomosis process.
The surgeon focuses on the terminal ileum, identifying an avascular plane near the ileocolic pedicle. A space is created transmesenterically, separating the tissue layers.
The surgeon continues working within an avascular plane near the terminal ileum, maintaining careful dissection around the ileocolic pedicle.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers around the ileocolic pedicle, ensuring that significant vasculature is not disrupted during the procedure.
The surgeon extends the peritoneal component of the mesentery, creating a visible void to facilitate the passage of the conduit.
The surgeon safely navigates the conduit through the mesentery to the opposite peritoneal side, maintaining a bloodless field during the creation of this passage.
The surgeon carefully mobilizes the terminal ileum, ensuring it is freed from any lateral attachments.
The surgeon enlarges the defect in the mesentery to facilitate the passage of the conduit, ensuring it can move through the created void.
The surgeon manipulates the terminal ileum to pass the conduit and anvil through the previously created defect in the mesentery.
The conduit is visibly passing down to the pelvis, ensuring a tension-free anastomosis within the surgical field.
The surgeon inserts a circular stapler gun into the rectum and introduces a spike, preparing for anastomosis.
The surgeon performs the anastomosis, ensuring the conduit is correctly oriented to prevent twisting, completing the connection between the bowel segments.
The surgeon performs an air leak test on the anastomosis to visually confirm its integrity, ensuring no air escapes from the surgical site.
